seasonality: Cold or windy days should shorten outdoor river segments and add indoor architecture or museum stops.
Warm-weather days support longer Riverwalk and skyline-view routes.
transport_advice: Downtown architecture and river stops are best handled as a compact walking and public-transit cluster.
Avoid pairing a river architecture route with distant neighborhoods unless the traveler wants a packed schedule.
planning_tips: Use the Chicago Riverwalk as the anchor for architecture requests and build nearby landmarks around it.
Keep one major observation deck or skyline stop rather than stacking multiple paid views.
Pair architecture walks with nearby Loop dining or cultural stops to limit backtracking.
title: Chicago Architecture, River, and Downtown Landmark Planning Notes
content: Seed benchmark knowledge for Chicago architecture routes: the river, Loop, and nearby landmarks form a strong first-time cluster. A realistic plan should respect weather exposure along the river and avoid sending travelers to distant neighborhoods during a compact architecture day.

district: Museum Campus, Grant Park, The Loop
seasonality: Winter and rainy days are good fits for museums with shorter park or lakefront breaks.
Clear summer days support adding Millennium Park or Grant Park between indoor visits.
transport_advice: Museum Campus and Loop museum routes should be grouped with nearby parks rather than split across the city.
Leave buffer time for transit between Museum Campus, Grant Park, and hotel areas.
planning_tips: Choose one or two major museums, then use nearby parks or lakefront breaks for pacing.
Avoid trying to complete every Museum Campus venue and the Art Institute in a single short day.
Use indoor museum options as weather backups for lakefront or park-heavy requests.
title: Chicago Museums, Parks, and Culture Planning Notes
content: Seed benchmark knowledge for Chicago museum routes: Museum Campus, the Art Institute, Millennium Park, and Grant Park can support a culture-heavy itinerary when the planner limits the number of major indoor venues and uses nearby parks for pacing.

district: West Loop, Pilsen, Chinatown, Logan Square
seasonality: Winter food routes should keep walking distances shorter and transit return simple.
Summer evenings can support patio dining and longer neighborhood walks.
transport_advice: Pick one or two food neighborhoods and avoid zigzagging across Chicago for unrelated meals.
Plan the evening return around CTA access or rideshare availability from the final dining neighborhood.
planning_tips: For food requests, preserve flexible time for restaurants, markets, and neighborhood wandering.
Pair West Loop dining with nearby downtown or Fulton Market time rather than distant lakefront museums.
Use Pilsen, Chinatown, or Logan Square as focused neighborhood anchors when the traveler asks for local flavor.
title: Chicago Food, Neighborhoods, and Citywalk Planning Notes
content: Seed benchmark knowledge for Chicago food routes: the strongest food itineraries choose a specific neighborhood or corridor, then keep transportation simple. West Loop, Pilsen, Chinatown, Logan Square, and River North can each anchor a different food-focused plan.
